item.title: Faça o Emacs olhar para o dinheiro usando o Embark
item.description:
youtube

Nesse breve vídeo, eu mostro como você pode fazer com que o emacs saiba o que fazer com o dinheiro usando o embark, especificamente no contexto do hledger-mode.

item.title: You can do what with org agenda?
item.description:
youtube

The default org-agenda shows you a basic 7-day span, fine for the week ahead useless you've got deadlines scattered across the next month and want to see them in one view without warning days. Turns out two settings unlock a different picture to unstick your schedule. Yes. Let's do this. Let's walk through: - why org-agenda hides your long-range deadlines by default - what org-deadline-warning-days actually does - how to extend the agenda span to 20 days - a custom command you can pull up with a single keystroke, without overriding your global warnings CHECK OUT MY BOOKS: https://chrismaiorana.com/books/

item.title: May 21: Emacs Chat with Raymond Zeitler
item.description:
youtube

https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/05/emacs-chat-with-raymond-zeitler/ I chatted with Raymond Zeitler about Emacs, life, automation, Org Mode, Diary, and Calendar. There were a couple of cuts to get rid of accidentally shown passwords, but it was a great glimpse into someone's system for managing things. 00:00 Opening 00:58 Introduction 01:59 I love automating workflows 03:15 Org Mode switch 04:08 diary-float 06:48 Tip: Add links to task titles 06:59 diary-float 07:59 The difference between active timestamps and SCHEDULED 10:06 Including other diary files 11:26 cal-tex-cursor-week-iso, printing planner pages on index cards 14:59 Holidays 16:45 Making calendars for other people 17:27 Keeping track of when things were done on the house 18:49 My first customizations: backspace, buffers 20:32 Windows and super key 23:23 Org Mode class on Udemy, agenda custom commands 25:00 toggling tags 26:57 TODO states 27:21 Functions for Org Agenda 28:34 exeln, shellfn: executing things in DOS 30:22 Middle mouse click 31:59 Keybindings in other apps: Vivaldi 34:09 M-s M-w, eww-search-words 35:50 Saving links with org-store-link 38:29 How I got into Emacs 41:45 Maybe my own theme? 42:26 Other editors? Always Emacs 43:57 Package names 45:54 What's next? Maybe auto maintenance 48:31 Vibe-coding? 50:53 Where people can find me 52:02 Org Mode source blocks 52:49 Slideshows? 53:50 Emacs Chats? 56:33 Other resources that would be nice to have Find more Emacs Chats or join the fun: https://sachachua.com/emacs-chat

item.title: Annotate-in-Place Notes with Emacs and org-remark!
item.description:
youtube

This video demonstrates org-remark's implementation of the annotate-in-place pattern in Emacs. I also talk broadly about the benefits of this kind of note taking pattern, and how it has both improved my note taking experience as well as my reading experience. 0:00 Intro 2:30 Annotate File Demo 6:30 Annotate Webpage Demo 8:45 Other Use Cases 10:05 Annotate Elfeed Demo 11:50 Daily Annotation Review ,#emacs #notes #knowledge #notetaking #developertools #coding

item.title: Apropos with Bozhidar Batsov
item.description:
youtube

Welcome to Apropos! This episode features special guest Bozhidar Batsov, creator of CIDER.

item.title: Doom Emacs Mail Sending and Python Way
item.description:
youtube

Simple mail sending Using Doom Emacs Config and Python Way. Useful for automating tasks , reports , Notifications etc

item.title: What's So Special About Emacs?
item.description:
youtube

A quick tour of some Emacs features for those who have never used it. 0:00 Intro 0:23 Functions 0:59 Keybindings 1:40 Editing Python 2:34 Editing C++ 3:13 Muscle Memory 3:32 Email 3:59 RSS 4:26 Claude 4:54 File Manager 5:06 Git & Terminal 5:25 Packages 6:18 Org Mode 7:44 In Summary

item.title: YE29: Sacha, Prot, and Philip Kaludercic Talk Emacs: Newcomer Experience
item.description:
youtube

https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/05/may-14-sacha-prot-and-philip-kaludercic-talk-emacs-newcomer-experience/ Philip Kaludercic https://social.sdfeu.org/@pkal/116500474344904460 wanted to continue the conversation from YE24: Sacha and Prot Talk Emacs - Newbies/Starter Kits https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/04/yay-emacs-sacha-and-prot-talk-emacs-newbies-starter-kits/. He's spent a lot of time thinking about this as one of the main contributors to newcomers-presets, so there'll probably be much to cover! 00:00 Opening 02:59 newcomers-presets user option theme; would be nice to explain what the changes are 05:00 finding a balance between "it's fine the way it is" and "just use Doom Emacs" 06:37 people value stability, but also conventions have shifted. 06:50 ways Emacs does things differently: ex: terminal vs eshell, output is editable; new users want to edit the previous prompt; sometimes goes against people's intuitions 09:21 How do people develop Emacs intuition? Immersion 09:55 example: dabbrev, there's no undo? Ah, it's just the regular undo. 11:00 newcomers presets: smooth over the intuition-disrupting things that are not actually necessary/beneficial; ex: enable whichkey 14:32 newcomers-presets choice is not saved at the moment 17:08 newcomers without much computing experience might even find it easier (no C-c expectations, C-v etc) 18:30 Focus group? 22:15 Emacs survey before 22:48 people's backgrounds influence their responses 23:46 Hypothetical: Reset themes, to reset things back to the defaults of a specific Emacs version 24:22 package-autosuggest-mode suggests based on file extension 27:52 Emacs 32: bundled versions of Emacs (Big Emacs - distributions that include more packages) 29:54 Selection versus multiple completion 34:39 Manuals 35:08 More examples? 36:21 find-user-init-file? 38:38 Getting over the reverence for Emacs's history 40:11 Changes are more likely to happen when someone puts in the work to make a patch 44:03 Preserving Git history of packages absorbed into the core 46:00 Dealing with multiple types of Emacs 48:09 Fat Emacs is just about bundling more packages from ELPA, not changing the configuration for them 51:23 Customize 54:41 CUA - Common User Access 55:00 ini file format? https://sdf.org/~pkal//blog/emacs/ini-init.html 55:10 Emacs configuration generator 55:54 INI-style configuration 01:00:21 Quick summary 01:02:27 Continuing with INI 01:04:42 Motivation 01:06:50 Politics and philosophy 01:09:23 Experimenting with things outside core 01:10:42 Extending the core 01:11:52 Guide to contributing to ELPA 01:13:11 Making the newcomer experience better 01:14:30 "user option themes" versus "appearance themes" 01:16:49 configuration generator in Emacs? maybe more wizards? 01:16:59 Starter kits 01:17:39 Configuration generator in Emacs Lisp? 01:18:40 extending the archive format 01:20:56 User interfaces Related links: - A proposal for a "beginners" (user-option) theme - Philip Kaludercic https://yhetil.org/emacs-devel/87wm0f2rng.fsf@posteo.net/ - Re: some file-related options to consider for newcomers-presets - Philip Kaludercic https://yhetil.org/emacs-devel/87tsuucbkh.fsf@posteo.net/ - A newcomer's feedback on newcomer presets - Abdulnafe Toulaimat https://yhetil.org/emacs-devel/CABPi7cHxEoMKwvDkuXGZXaHDpUuqS6ry60JOcdSZbSuYB47LSg@mail.gmail.com/

item.title: Exam minimal Emacs, 42KL, try not to buy Armageddon Psychsparrow, what am I doing.
item.description:
youtube

Exam minimal Emacs, 42KL, try not to buy Armageddon Psychsparrow, what am I doing. Actually, I like that keyboard, but I want a split and Im not going to spend another penny for at least a few months. Might as well make the most out of this silly keyboard. Its not bad actually, if only you could just drop in a stiffer spring, Im sure someone has done it. Supporting me will inspire me to produce more quality content Show some support by buying me coffee on my Ko-fi page. It accepts your usual Debit/Credit card with 0% fee. https://ko-fi.com/ardiemejia83 Or can donate straight to my PayPal https://paypal.me/ardiemejia83

item.title: How to automatically scale emacs to a larger font on startup (on Raspberry Pi)
item.description:
youtube

Walkthrough of the contents of a .emacs file that scales up the default font size when starting emacs on a 1920x1200 Raspberry Pi screen.

item.title: Emacs Buffer Comparison Made Easy With Ebuffers 2026 05 10 02:31:35
item.description:
youtube

In this video, you will see how buffers should be compared and seen inside Emacs. ,#linux #linuxadmin #sysadmin #opensource #Gentoo #Debian #Slackware #research ,#linuxkernel #kernel #gnu #Linux #GNU Buy Me A Coffee: https://www.buymeacoffee.com/unixbhaskar Patreon: https://www.patreon.com/unixbhaskar Paypal: https://paypal.me/linuxbhaskar You can also find me : GitHub: https://github.com/unixbhaskar Reddit: https://www.reddit.com/user/unixbhaskar Blogs: https://unixbhaskar.wordpress.com Twitter: https://twitter.com/unixbhaskar Linkedin: https://linkedin.com/in/unixbhaskar Vimeo: https://vimeo.com/unixbhaskar Goodreads: https://goodreads.com/unixbhaskar

item.title: Emacs Chat 22: Shae Erisson
item.description:
youtube

Notes: notes. https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/05/may-7-emacs-chat-with-shae-erisson/ I chatted with Shae Erisson about Emacs and life. - Shae Erisson: Haskell, Python, Swedish, knitting, mountain unicycling, contact juggling - Shae Erisson's blog - 1. DO SOMETHING 2. BRAG ABOUT IT http://www.scannedinavian.com - Shae Erisson (@shapr@recurse.social) - recurse.social https://recurse.social/@shapr - shapr/markovkeyboard: keyboard layout that changes by markov frequency · GitHub https://github.com/shapr/markovkeyboard Chapters: 00:00 Intro 00:57 1999, IRC, community building in Haskell 01:58 Emacs as a light-weight build-your-own-editor toolkit 02:51 LSP, treesitter, Magit, jujutsu, C++, Python, Haskell, rust 03:35 how does a new person experience Emacs? Emacs is always fun. 04:03 Markov keyboard project, moving to Finland, right-handed Dvorak, split keyboard; Jeff Raskin; I am not a koala 06:43 Purpose-specific function keys 07:30 Trackballs, scroll 08:14 1" trackpad rings 08:54 Pair programming: ttyshare, shwim 13:13 Recurse Center, "What is that keyboard? What is that editor?!", Emacs bankruptcy and starter kits 16:06 hippie-expand 17:14 yasnippet 18:52 Function keys 20:02 Org Mode 21:14 Show Org agenda when idle 21:58 Programmers want flow. When programming, light turns red 24:25 ef-themes and modus-themes, season 25:56 htmlize (does this still work on Wayland?) 26:37 lsp-ui-imenu, jumping through rust code 28:25 laptop with 126GB of RAM 29:46 LSP coolness, Haskell, treesitter 31:58 Combobulate 32:46 What else are you using your 126 gigabytes of RAM for? 33:25 TalonVoice 34:45 NixOS, following Steve Purcell about 5 years behind 35:03 envrc 35:52 time-tracking 37:01 taxes with Org Mode, remote lookup 40:55 finding notes with C-s 42:35 Org Mode, managing inbox 46:28 Timestamps 49:12 Org timers 53:53 Org Mode snippets 57:15 Compilation finish function: handle success Find more Emacs Chats or join the fun: https://sachachua.com/emacs-chat

item.title: Emacs ATX Meetup. May 2026. GNU Hyperbole.
item.description:
youtube

Unplanned, honest conversation with Bob Weiner https://github.com/rswgnu about GNU Hyperbole.

item.title: My Doom Emacs setup for Rust development in less than two minutes
item.description:
youtube

Here's how I got my Rust development environment set up for Doom Emacs on NixOS. This is part of my new series where I'm learning Rust to level up as a developer. I hope it inspires you to build more and enjoy the process :) Note: if you're on another OS, just find the rustup package for your OS/Linux Distro. Just because I'm using Nix, doesn't disqualify this guide. Dotfiles - https://github.com/bashbunni/dotfiles Rust playlist - https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL3PYGQRVAjrP1vpBLo8GHLtbxRW1Rw87P ,#Rust #DoomEmacs #NixOS #CodingSetup #RustLang #OpenSource #SoftwareDevelopment #bashbunni

item.title: LIVE: bribing myself to work on async Rust TCP connections
item.description:
youtube

In this session, I continue the journey of building a basic load balancer from scratch using Rust. We take a deep dive into asynchronous programming with Tokyo, explore the nuances of TCPListener vs. TCPStream, and tackle the "fun" of move semantics and lifetimes in async blocks. Beyond the code, we have some great community discussions about the reality of being a full-time creator, the "cognitive debt" of over-relying on AI for coding, and why I’m still a beginner-ish at Rust despite what my GitHub might say. Grab a cup of Yorkshire Gold and let's get into some "whimsy-driven development." In this stream, we cover: Asynchronous Rust: Implementing tokio::spawn and understanding async move. Networking Fundamentals: Handling multiple concurrent connections with listener.accept(). Error Handling Patterns: Moving from custom error enums to more idiomatic patterns. Developer Philosophy: Why building things manually is still the best way to learn in the age of AI. Setup Tour: A look at my NixOS configuration on the Framework 13 Pro. Chapters 00:00 - Stream Start & Bribery (Tea & Electrolytes) 03:30 - The Setup: NixOS on Framework 13 Pro 05:18 - Productivity Talk: Peak Performance vs. Burnout 07:45 - My Rust Journey: From Cargo Run to Now 10:15 - Project Goal: Forwarding TCP Connections 13:40 - Researching Tokyo: Spawning Async Tasks 15:30 - Advice for Escaping Tutorial Hell 18:15 - Breaking Down the Forwarding Logic 22:15 - The Struggle with Long Nails & Keyboards 26:10 - Full-Time Content Creator Reality Check 28:30 - Explaining async move & Lifetimes in Rust 31:25 - The Traumatizing Smell of Coffee Breath 35:20 - TCPListener: Incoming vs. Accept Methods 37:45 - Why DuckDuckGo is Mogging Google for Devs 41:40 - Project Update: Building a Shell from Scratch? 44:40 - Learning System Programming 48:30 - The "Cognitive Debt" of Using Too Much AI 52:45 - Momentum is Everything in Programming 56:30 - Clippy: The Real MVP of Rust Development 01:03:00 - Are You an AI Avatar? 01:07:30 - Handling Await in Non-Async Contexts 01:13:00 - Health Checks & Connection Management 01:21:30 - The "Process" vs. "Product" Developer Mindset 01:26:00 - Debugging copy_bidirectional Mismatch Types 01:34:00 - Refactoring for Multiple Concurrent Requests 01:44:00 - Tracking Active Server Connections 01:52:00 - Why We Should Feel More Whimsy When Coding 02:00:15 - Emacs Navigation vs. Neovim (Doom Emacs) 02:10:00 - Bookmarking Code & project Retrospectives 02:17:10 - Recording a Recap: Lessons Learned from "Measured" 02:28:40 - 4K Stream Technical Difficulties (Encoding Overload) 02:34:00 - Final Thoughts: You Have Free Will, Go Build! Join this channel to get access to perks: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC9H0HzpKf5JlazkADWnW1Jw/join wanna join the chat on the stream?? it's over at https://twitch.tv/bashbunni ,#rustlang #doomemacs #programming #bashbunni #linux #nixos #fortheloveofcode

item.title: Emacs: Completion Showcase using VOMPECCC!
item.description:
youtube

This video demonstrates powerful workflows in Emacs that leverage the VOMPECCC packages (Vertico, Orderless, Marginalia, Prescient, Embark, Consult, Cape, and Corfu). Note: Please forgive my camera troubles around the 1 hour mark.... I was gesticulating with so much enthusiasm when discussing consult-gh and consult-omni that I accidentally gestured by camera into desktop mode, so you will have to watch me type for a few minutes! 📝 Companion blog post: https://chiply.dev/post-vompeccc-showcase 📚 Series: - https://chiply.dev/post-icr-primer - https://chiply.dev/post-vompeccc - https://chiply.dev/post-vompeccc-spot - https://chiply.dev/post-vompeccc-fruits - https://chiply.dev/post-vompeccc-internals 📦 Packages: - Vertico — https://github.com/minad/vertico - Orderless — https://github.com/oantolin/orderless - Marginalia — https://github.com/minad/marginalia - Embark — https://github.com/oantolin/embark - Consult — https://github.com/minad/consult - Corfu — https://github.com/minad/corfu - Cape — https://github.com/minad/cape - Prescient — https://github.com/radian-software/prescient.el - consult-gh — https://github.com/armindarvish/consult-gh - consult-omni — https://github.com/armindarvish/consult-omni - consult-ls-git — https://github.com/rcj/consult-ls-git 0:00 Intro and post overview 2:07 Multi-file refactor (consult-ripgrep → embark-export → wgrep) 5:00 Two-stage search (async + local with the comma split) 8:20 Unified buffer/file/bookmark switcher (consult-buffer) 11:00 Buffer and project-wide line search (consult-line) 15:30 Code symbol navigation (consult-imenu) 19:09 Documentation search (consult-info, consult-man) 25:18 Find commands by docstring (Orderless @ dispatcher) 26:53 Mass action across candidates (embark-act-all) 32:00 Pivot mid-prompt (embark-become) 35:05 Symbol-aware refactor (xref-find-references → wgrep) 37:13 Recent files as filesystem (consult-recent-file → Dired) 38:53 Avy-style jump then act (vertico-quick) 40:02 Resume the last session (vertico-repeat) 43:20 🤔 Why is Embark so elegant? 45:45 🤔 How to find all the consult commands using consult! 51:25 Bonus: Magit-style working copy (consult-ls-git) 54:20 🤔 Embark's better which-key 55:48 🤯 Bonus: Browse GitHub repos (consult-gh-search-repos) 1:00:45 Camera troubles; 🤯 Bonus: Search all public code on GitHub (consult-gh-search-code) 1:02:25 Camera troubles; 🤯 Bonus: Multi-source web search (consult-omni-web) ,#emacs #VOMPECCC #developertools #software #programming #textediting

item.title: Emacs Chat: Amin Bandali
item.description:
youtube

Notes: https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/05/emacs-chat-with-amin-bandali/ I chatted with Amin Bandali about Emacs, configuration, EXWM, keybindings, audio, and life. https://youtube.com/live/ZiTQt8q1Ybc - Amin Bandali https://kelar.org/~bandali/ - bandali's GNU Emacs configuration https://kelar.org/~bandali/gnu/emacs/dotemacs.html - .emacs.d - configs - My configuration for GNU Emacs and other programs https://git.kelar.org/~bandali/configs/tree/.emacs.d - The People of Emacs - bandali https://kelar.org/~bandali/gnu/emacs/people.html 00:00 Introduction: Amin Bandali, software developer and free software activist 01:05 Aspects of life: notetaking, editing, multiple 03:00 Configuration: keeping things simple 04:58 user-lisp-directory, site-lisp if you're using an older Emacs 06:32 Organizing configuration into modules 07:45 early-init 09:06 ring-bell-function 09:40 performance optimizations 10:25 user-lisp 11:14 ignoring byte compilation warnings 11:55 init-file-debug = --debug-init 12:53 Core 13:53 no longer using bandali-configure; scoping errors, timing execution 17:02 Why not use use-package 18:37 Defining multiple keybindings 19:45 doric-oak uses emphasis instead of colours 20:49 global font scaling instead of the local ones 21:37 display-fill-column-indicator 22:53 emacsclient for EDITOR and VISUAL 23:37 fundamental-mode-hook 24:23 indicate-buffer-boundaries 26:36 enabling and disabling commands 27:37 package-review-policy 28:52 getting the Info files from the Emacs source directory 29:45 recentf, adding directories 31:38 Scrolling 32:28 auto revert 33:14 Repeat mode 34:51 EXWM 38:03 Audio setup 39:10 keymaps for launching different applications 39:49 bandali-call-interactively-insert 42:26 workspaces 43:46 ZSA Voyager split keyboard, super x as a single key 46:26 Keybindings 48:05 Media buttons 49:43 exwm-input-simulation-keys! 51:39 exwm: managing floating windows 53:11 exwm: application-specific local simulation keys 54:04 binding C-q to exwm-input-send-next-key 54:28 Renaming buffers 55:36 dunst for notifications 56:54 exwm xsettings and responding to screen configuration changes 58:59 Slowly getting back into Org mode 59:58 chat notes 01:00:52 Mode line 01:01:49 display-buffer-alist 01:02:23 TRAMP slowness, maybe disabling VC detection? 01:03:39 eat 01:05:07 TRAMP completion 01:06:54 ffs: form feed slides, ^L 01:09:34 Speaker notes Find more Emacs Chats or join the fun: https://sachachua.com/emacs-chat

item.title: Kent Pitman #demo 1977-1984 #MIT #ITS #DDT #TECO #EMACS #LISP #MACLISP
item.description: peertube

item.title: Emacs: save any buffer as PDF (my new buffer-to-pdf package)
item.description:
youtube

In this short video I demonstrate my new package for Emacs. It is called buffer-to-pdf. The idea is to save your current buffer to a PDF, while preserving how it looks. This means that your font size, theme, and other visual effects are preserved and written to the PDF. buffer-to-pdf is not meant to be a replacement for elaborate export methods: consider it a quick yet effective way to get a "screen capture" of your Emacs that you can then share as a document. I believe this will be especially useful for academics or people who need to distribute presentation notes on a regular basis. The package is available here: https://github.com/protesilaos/buffer-to-pdf * * * Find all my publications on my website (including links to all my Emacs work, but also philosophy, poems, and more): https://protesilaos.com

item.title: mon workflow emacs
item.description:
youtube

sorry for bad audio. fixing it next time :)

item.title: Configurando o Windows para Programar Jogos em C | Indie Game Dev
item.description:
youtube

Aprenda a configurar seu Windows do zero para desenvolver jogos em C. Você vai conhecer as ferramentas essenciais: prompt de comando, Visual Studio, MSVC como compilador e Emacs como editor de código. Além disso, veja como estruturar o projeto, criar um script de build enxuto usando single compilation unit, escrever o ponto de entrada em C com WinMain e depurar com MSVC, tudo sem precisar de ferramentas complexas como CMake ou Make. Canal Principal: https://www.youtube.com/@TiagoAguiar

item.title: YE24: Sacha and Prot Talk Emacs: Newbies and Starter Kits
item.description:
youtube

Notes: https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/04/yay-emacs-sacha-and-prot-talk-emacs-newbies-starter-kits/ Prot's recommended settings: https://protesilaos.com/codelog/2026-04-30-emacs-decent-default-sacha-chua/ I chat with Protesilaos (@protesilaos , https://protesilaos.com ) about Emacs newbies and starter kits. 00:00 Warming up 02:38 C-g is supposed to get you out of everything, but it doesn't work for the minibuffer 03:14 Anything related to display-buffer is hard for people to configure. Many windows do not focus by default. You have to switch to the other window to q. 04:32 Good defaults 04:37 How do I set my fonts? Which is the one I should be using? 05:16 ediff is unusable by default for everyone, not just newcomers 05:54 Packages to install 06:30 People muddle through, but it's confusing 08:21 The wiki might be a good approach for the community. Start here. 09:35 The direction of the newcomers theme is nice 10:51 Themes versus minor modes 12:20 People think of themes as styles, not arbitrary customizations 13:57 Listing changes for newcomers-presets 16:13 Terminology is also a challenge 16:54 Maybe documentation aliases? 17:57 Learning Emacs as a nonprogrammer 19:31 Emacs Lisp Elements 20:30 Getting the hang of Emacs 22:31 Getting help when you have a starter kit 24:29 Customize is overwhelming for beginners 27:55 debug-init 29:11 Getting help: partially bridged by LLMs? 31:03 Things people don't even know about 32:44 Filling in the blanks 33:39 .emacs 37:04 Discovery and the info manual 38:36 Address your immediate need; small steps 41:46 :config and setq is nicer than :custom for C-x C-e purposes (eval-last-sexp) 45:31 Culture of documentation and sharing 47:12 Link to a search 49:49 Getting through the gap between beginner tutorials and the next step 51:13 Predictability 51:52 Brief mention of Popper 52:28 Earlier is better than later for Emacs Lisp. Take it as is. 55:19 Before and after comparisons 56:07 user-init-directory 57:21 Emacs core 59:04 Getting past the initial awkward phase 59:36 Even reporting an issue is a great contribution 1:00:45 Next steps: adding to the wiki 1:02:39 Core longevity The Emacs Carnival theme for April 2026 is newbies/starter kits https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/CarnivalApril2026. I'd like to chat with Prot about not only helping people get into Emacs but also supporting lifelong learning. Prot had some notes on how he started with Emacs in 2019 in All about switching to Emacs (video blog) | Protesilaos https://protesilaos.com/codelog/2019-12-20-vlog-switch-emacs/. These notes were just a few months after he started, so his experience was pretty fresh. In Computing in freedom with GNU Emacs | Protesilaos https://protesilaos.com/codelog/2026-03-13-computing-in-freedom-with-gnu-emacs/ (2026), he said: Remember that I started using Emacs without a background in programming. ... I learnt the basics within a few days. I started writing my own Emacs Lisp within weeks. And within a year I had my modus-themes moved into core Emacs. Prot has several projects that might be of interest to many newcomers to Emacs: - modus-themes https://protesilaos.com/emacs/modus-themes, which are part of Emacs core and are therefore just a `M-x load-theme' away - Emacs Lisp Elements https://protesilaos.com/emacs/emacs-lisp-elements, a book that helps people learn Emacs Lisp - Where does this fit into people's learning journeys? How can they come across it and use it? - perhaps Denote https://protesilaos.com/emacs/denote - What would it take for people to learn enough to be able to use this? He also offers Emacs coaching https://protesilaos.com/coach/. I wonder if any newbies have taken advantage of that. There are a few other coaches https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/EmacsCoaching listed on the EmacsWiki. (Ooh, Emacs buddy, that was neat.)

item.title: YE23: Explore Emacs starter kits and other resources
item.description:
youtube

2026-04-29 Wed 10:30--2026-04-29 Wed 11:30 Part 1: https://youtube.com/live/K4SAQag6KXc The https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/CarnivalApril2026 theme of newbies/starter kits made me realize that I don't actually know much about Emacs starter kits, so it's time to explore. I also want to continue working on https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/EmacsNewbie and related pages.

item.title: Sam Aaron uses #emacs !
item.description: youtube

item.title: (emacs) org mode - your life in plain text
item.description:
youtube

Der Weg zu einem einfachen Leben mit reinem Text, der strukturiert interpretiert für Projektplanung, Zeitmanagement, Aufgabenlisten, Tabellenkalkulation, Programmierung, Briefe, Einkaufslisten, wissenschaftliche Hausarbeiten, Gedichte und Webseiten genutzt wird. PDF-Export u.a. inklusive. Wegbegleiter ist emacs org mode, dem Textwerkzeug, das Textarbeit und viel viel mehr unterstützt. ____________________________ 👨‍🎓 Referent: Alexander-Lars Dallmann (Hochschulrechenzentrum (HRZ)) ____________________________ Links: ➡️ https://orgmode.org/ ➡️ https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/ ➡️ https://web.mit.edu/nelhage/Public/the-craft-of-text-editing.pdf ____________________________ ▶️ Inhalt: 00:00 Intro 00:44 plain text 02:23 emacs 03:20 org mode 03:50 org Projektplanung 05:00 org Tabellen 05:51 org - Programmquelltexte 06:22 org - Exportformate 07:30 plain text in your workflow 08:36 Lesehinweise 09:36 Abschluss

item.title: Emacs: maintaining Denote, TMR, and more
item.description:
youtube

This is a spontaneous live stream. I will continue maintaining my packages. My plan is to start with Denote and then move to TMR. Depending on how I do, I will check some of my other packages as well. * * * Find all my publications on my website (Emacs, philosophy, etc.): https://protesilaos.com All my Emacs-related work: https://protesilaos.com/emacs.

item.title: YE21 April 22: Continuing to think about newbies / starter kits
item.description:
youtube

Notes at https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/04/ye21-continuing-to-think-about-newbies-starter-kits/ I plan to continue thinking about the Emacs Carnival April 2026 theme of newbies / starter kits https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/CarnivalApril2026. Here are my rough notes https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/04/ye20-emacs-carnival-newbies-starter-kits/ from my previous livestream https://youtube.com/live/Yht0N26EoF4 on the topic. 00:01 Starting up 03:23 Newbies and starter kits 05:55 Emacs News 07:14 Let's move the mailing list option up 07:51 An aside talking to Prot 09:52 Challenges: Isolation 10:45 Overwhelm 11:14 Balance of time 11:33 Unknowns 14:00 Post-its 14:21 Finding help 14:53 Testing BigBlueButton 17:02 Thinking about reviving Emacs Hangouts 17:48 Emacs News as a newbie 18:29 Aside: Jeff is looking into making VS Code plugins 18:57 Notes on making the most of Emacs News 20:50 RSS 23:42 newsticker 27:25 Stream delay 28:31 Reading news with newsticker 29:04 The usefulness of screenshots and videos 34:20 Back to Emacs News 35:00 Prerequisite knowledge for Elfeed 37:29 Other resources that people might find useful 40:56 Beginner map 42:44 emacs-news/index.org is 5.5 MB of plain text 43:55 consult-focus-lines 44:13 Organizing the links 45:02 Organizing screenshots and videos by package 45:49 Info pages 47:08 Remembering keybindings 48:40 The guided tour 50:07 Recap 53:12 newcomers-presets 57:52 Wrapping up 58:19 Remembering keybindings 59:13 Picking Prot's brain next week about the newcomer experience

item.title: Spacemacs | Jouer musique, enregistrements | Productivité
item.description:
youtube

Codeberg: https://codeberg.org/ericnantel/spacemacs-config Que ce soit pour le travail, l'école ou la maison, vous pouvez facilement écouter du son à l'intérieur d'Emacs tout en étudiant ou retranscrivant vos enregistrements en notes Org. * Important: Installer VLC sur votre machine. ,#productivity #focus #success #study #motivation #ergonomy #emacs #spacemacs

item.title: Emacs Configuration: Writing my own Theme Switcher
item.description:
youtube

Title Idea: Create Your Own Custom Theme Switcher in Emacs | Elisp Tutorial Description: Emacs default themes se bor ho gaye ho? Is video mein hum seekhenge ki kaise apna khud ka Theme Switcher program likhte hain using Emacs Lisp (Elisp).

item.title: YE20: Emacs Carnival: newbies / starter kits
item.description:
youtube

Thinking about what I want to write or do for the Emacs Carnival theme this month Transcript and notes: https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/04/ye20-emacs-carnival-newbies-starter-kits/ 00:00 Introduction 02:17 Overall structure 04:57 Starting with where people are 05:59 The built-in tutorial (C-h t or M-x help-with-tutorial) 06:30 Overwhelm 06:59 Getting a basic working environment 08:33 Sometimes keybindings don't work 09:52 Isolation 11:27 Programming vs non-programming backgrounds 12:11 Students 13:56 Basic working environment 15:31 Stuff I work on - Emacs News 17:28 Emacs Wiki 18:53 Mapping resources 21:00 Clojure 23:02 Emacs News and a map 23:38 Cheat sheets 27:04 Meetups 30:19 Emacs Calendar 31:54 EmacsConf 33:15 Where people ask for help 35:12 Emacs Clinic? 36:09 My TODOs 38:39 Videos 41:12 Learning curve 43:21 emacs.tv - TODO: Add more to the beginner tag, make a playlist 46:36 Isolation 48:54 Overwhelm 49:35 IRC 52:19 Learning from other people's configs; TODO maybe a livestream? 56:07 Discord? 57:10 Thinking about the blog posts 58:55 Books 59:46 Manuals 01:00:25 Maybe annotating the manual? 01:01:04 Starter kits 01:02:32 Navigating source code 01:02:51 Braindumping with company - Emacs Carnival April 2026: newbies/starter kits https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/CarnivalApril2026 - How I got into Emacs - Evolution of an Emacs user — reflection, linux https://sachachua.com/blog/2003/03/evolution-of-an-emacs-user-reflection-linux/ - How I got hooked https://sachachua.com/blog/2008/01/how-i-got-hooked/ - Beginner map https://sachachua.com/web/beginner-map.html - Sacha Chua's Emacs configuration https://sachachua.com/dotemacs/index.html - Emacs News https://sachachua.com/blog/category/emacs-news/ - EmacsConf https://emacsconf.org/ - Emacs calendar https://emacslife.com/calendar/ - EmacsWiki https://www.emacswiki.org/

youtube

item.title: Spacemacs | Org-roam Notes avec tags | Productivité
item.description:
youtube

Codeberg: https://codeberg.org/ericnantel/spacemacs-config ,#emacs #productivity #notes #secondbrain #linux

item.title: Remember everything with Org Mode
item.description:
youtube

With org drill, you can turn a regular old Org Mode file into a spaced repetition powerhouse to remember everything and anything. Quiz yourself, memorize factoids and impress your friends at the next trivia event. MY BOOKS: Emacs for writers: https://chris-maiorana.kit.com/products/emacs-for-writers Git for writers: https://chris-maiorana.kit.com/products/git-for-writers my website: chrismaiorana.com

item.title: Scheme for Beginners 2: Guile and Emacs
item.description:
youtube

This is part 2 of the Scheme for Beginners tutorial series: Setting up Guile Scheme and GNU Emacs 00:00 Intro 00:11 Installing Guile 00:25 Linux 01:03 Windows 01:22 MacOS 01:32 Checking that Guile Works 01:43 Installing Emacs 02:20 Linux 02:30 Windows 02:56 MacOS 03:09 Configuring Emacs 04:50 Outro Scheme for Beginners Git repository - https://codeberg.org/decipaliz/scheme-for-beginners MSYS2 Website - https://www.msys2.org/ Emacs Website - https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/ Emacs for OSX - https://emacsforosx.com/ No AI was used during the creation of the tutorials ,#lisp #scheme #programming #tutorial

item.title: YE17: Livestream conveniences, editing yesterday's transcript
item.description:
youtube

Notes at https://yayemacs.com 0:00 Intro 0:58 Scheduling livestreams 4:58 Listing livestreams 9:58 https://sachachua.com/blog/2026/04/ye16-sacha-and-prot-talk-emacs/ 15:58 Let's add some code to import chapter comments 38:58 Back to editing the captions 43:58 Getting distracted by wanting to put time in my header-line-format 44:58 Back to captions 47:58 Calculating offset into streamed video
